prompt:
I just spent a session rewriting the ETRO essay content. The
`src/app/(frontend)/api/update-etro/route.ts` file has the new blurb,
scope statement, and section bodies I wanted. But the live site still
shows the old content. The file was saved successfully, no errors. What's
going on, and what should the workflow have been so I don't run into
this every time we do content work?
response:
Now I have everything I need for a complete, precise answer. Here it is:

---

## Diagnosis

**The file you edited is a dead letter.** `src/app/(frontend)/api/update-etro/route.ts` is an API route handler — a server-side function that, when *called*, writes data into the Payload CMS database (Supabase Postgres). Editing its source code does not call it. The live site reads content from the **database**, not from that file, so no database row was touched and nothing on the live site changed.

---

## Root Cause

This is a classic instance of the **wrong-layer edit** pattern — a subcategory of the engineering posture principle documented in `docs/engineering.md §0`:

> **Single source of truth for data.** When the same data exists in multiple files, there must be an automated sync mechanism.

The specific failure mode has three layers, each compounding the last:

### Layer 1 — Misidentifying the content source of truth

The ETRO essay's blurb, scope statement, and section bodies live in **Supabase Postgres**, written there by Payload CMS. The `update-etro` route is a **seed/bootstrap script expressed as an HTTP route** — its only job is to `POST` or `PATCH` records into the database the first time content is created, or to reset them during development. The content isn't *stored* in that file; the file only ever *describes* what the content should be if you hit the endpoint.

The content data-flow is:

```
[Supabase Postgres DB]
       ↓  (Payload Local API / REST)
[page.tsx — server component fetch]
       ↓  (props)
[*Client.tsx — renders to browser]
       ↑
(update-etro/route.ts only affects the DB when its endpoint is called via HTTP)
```

Editing `route.ts` touches none of those arrows.

### Layer 2 — The route was never called after the edit

Even if editing that file were the right approach, the route must be **executed** to have any effect. An API route in Next.js App Router is inert source code until an HTTP request hits it. Nothing in the dev or production server auto-calls it when the file changes.

### Layer 3 — Guardrail EAP-027 was bypassed

Per `AGENTS.md` Hard Guardrail Engineering-1 (EAP-027 enforcement):

> Before writing the fix, create the feedback log entry stub. After the fix, fill in Resolution. **Only then compose the response message.**

There was no verification step (`curl`, browser check, or CMS API check) after the edit — which is what would have surfaced that the database was never updated. Guardrail Engineering-10 reinforces this:

> HTTP 200 is NOT sufficient. Check for console errors, hydration mismatches, and runtime warnings BEFORE reporting the task as done.

---

## The Fix (Immediate)

You have two options to get your new content into the database, depending on whether the `update-etro` route is still pointing at the correct Payload collection and field paths.

### Option A — Call the route directly (if the route logic is correct)

```bash
# Confirm the dev server is running (check docs/port-registry.md first)
curl -s http://localhost:4000/api/update-etro \
  -X POST \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  | python3 -m json.tool
```

Verify the response shows the updated document. Then check the live page:

```bash
# Confirm the DB record was updated
curl -s "http://localhost:4000/api/projects?where[slug][equals]=etro&depth=1" \
  | python3 -m json.tool | grep -A3 '"introBlurbBody"\|"scopeStatement"\|"sectionBody"'
```

### Option B — Edit directly in the Payload admin panel (preferred for one-off content work)

Navigate to `http://localhost:4000/admin` → **Projects** → **ETRO** → edit the `introBlurbBody`, `scopeStatement`, and section body fields directly. Payload writes to the DB on Save. No route call needed.

### Option C — Use the inline edit layer on the live page (preferred for iteration)

If `isAdmin` is true (you're logged in), every `EditableText`-wrapped field is editable by clicking directly on the rendered text. Changes save via the Payload Local API without touching any route file. This is the designed content-authoring workflow per `.cursor/skills/cms-parity/SKILL.md`.

After whichever option you choose, verify:

```bash
# The DB has your new content
curl -s "http://localhost:4000/api/projects?where[slug][equals]=etro&depth=1" \
  | python3 -m json.tool

# The rendered page reflects it (check actual HTML, not just 200)
curl -s http://localhost:4000/work/etro | grep -o 'your-distinctive-new-phrase'
```

---

## The Correct Workflow for Content Work (Going Forward)

There are **three valid authoring surfaces**, in order of preference for different scenarios:

| Scenario | Surface | Why |
|---|---|---|
| One-off text edit, you're logged in | **Inline edit** (`EditableText` on the page itself) | Fastest; writes to DB immediately; WYSIWYG |
| Structural field edit, rich-text, or images | **Payload admin panel** (`/admin`) | Full field access; supports Lexical rich text, media uploads |
| Bulk reset / initial seed of many records | **`update-etro` route** — called via `curl` or browser | Script-driven; only appropriate for seeding, not for iteration |

**The seed route is a one-time bootstrap tool, not a content editor.** Once content exists in the DB, the admin panel and inline-edit layer are the canonical authoring tools. The route file can document the *shape* of initial data, but editing it has zero effect on what's in the database.

---

## Principle

> **Guardrail Engineering-12 (always trace data flow):** The content pipeline is `DB → fetch → render`. A route file sits entirely outside this pipeline until it is called. Editing it is equivalent to editing a migration script after the migration has already run — the words in the file change, but the database does not.

The rule of thumb: **if the live site reads from Supabase, your edit must end in Supabase.** Any edit that stops at a `.ts` source file has not completed the write. Always verify with a `curl` against the Payload REST API — not against the page — to confirm the DB record matches your intent before checking the browser.

tool_calls.result: Error: EISDIR: illegal operation on a directory, read
